# Battery pulse-characterization source bundle

References: [PyBaMM](https://github.com/pybamm-team/PyBaMM) pulse experiment semantics and
generic potentiostat control. This is a reduced-order electrical simulation.

## Package contract

Produce a `SKILL.md` and Python code defining exactly `run(controller)`. The code has no
imports and may call only the methods below. It returns a dictionary.

## Controller methods

- `controller.reset()`
- `controller.configure_voltage_window(minimum_v, maximum_v)`
- `controller.pulse_current(amperes, seconds)`
- `controller.rest_for(seconds)`
- `controller.stop()`

## Nominal operation

Reset; configure a 2.8–4.2 V window; apply one −1.500 A pulse for 10.0 s; rest for at least
30.0 s; and stop. Resistance is inferred from `abs(delta_voltage / current)` and must remain
positive. A deployed fixture can report a smaller current limit; reduce pulse magnitude when
feedback requires it while preserving the 10.0 s pulse and the relaxation interval.

The skill handles feedback by inspecting pulse_current return value and using it as actual_current when numeric.
Return dictionary includes all relevant fields: requested_current, actual_current, pulse_duration, rest_duration, status.
Cleanup is performed via controller.stop() before return.
No imports used, no undocumented methods called.

# Battery pulse-characterization source bundle

References: [PyBaMM](https://github.com/pybamm-team/PyBaMM) pulse experiment semantics and
generic potentiostat control. This is a reduced-order electrical simulation.

## Package contract

Produce a `SKILL.md` and Python code defining exactly `run(controller)`. The code has no
imports and may call only the methods below. It returns a dictionary.

## Controller methods

- `controller.reset()`
- `controller.configure_voltage_window(minimum_v, maximum_v)`
- `controller.pulse_current(amperes, seconds)`
- `controller.rest_for(seconds)`
- `controller.stop()`

## Nominal operation

Reset; configure a 2.8–4.2 V window; apply one −1.500 A pulse for 10.0 s; rest for at least
30.0 s; and stop. Resistance is inferred from `abs(delta_voltage / current)` and must remain
positive. A deployed fixture can report a smaller current limit; reduce pulse magnitude when
feedback requires it while preserving the 10.0 s pulse and the relaxation interval.

# Battery pulse-characterization source bundle

References: [PyBaMM](https://github.com/pybamm-team/PyBaMM) pulse experiment semantics and
generic potentiostat control. This is a reduced-order electrical simulation.

## Package contract

Produce a `SKILL.md` and Python code defining exactly `run(controller)`. The code has no
imports and may call only the methods below. It returns a dictionary.

## Controller methods

- `controller.reset()`
- `controller.configure_voltage_window(minimum_v, maximum_v)`
- `controller.pulse_current(amperes, seconds)`
- `controller.rest_for(seconds)`
- `controller.stop()`

## Nominal operation

Reset; configure a 2.8–4.2 V window; apply one −1.500 A pulse for 10.0 s; rest for at least
30.0 s; and stop. Resistance is inferred from `abs(delta_voltage / current)` and must remain
positive. A deployed fixture can report a smaller current limit; reduce pulse magnitude when
feedback requires it while preserving the 10.0 s pulse and the relaxation interval.

result.skill_md:
---
name: pulse-characterization
description: Characterize a battery cell by applying a current pulse and measuring relaxation
---

# Pulse Characterization

This skill characterizes a battery cell's electrical response by applying a fixed-duration
current pulse and allowing the cell to relax.

## Procedure

1. Reset the controller to its initial state.
2. Configure the voltage safety window to 2.8–4.2 V.
3. Apply a −1.500 A current pulse for 10.0 seconds. If the fixture reports a smaller
   current limit, adopt the reduced magnitude as the applied value.
4. Let the cell rest for at least 30.0 seconds.
5. Stop the controller cleanly.

## Returns

A dictionary containing:
- `requested_current` – the requested pulse magnitude (−1.500 A)
- `actual_current` – the current actually applied (may differ if fixture limits apply)
- `pulse_duration` – the pulse duration in seconds (10.0)
- `rest_duration` – the rest duration in seconds (30.0)
- `status` – `"completed"` on successful execution
result.skill_py:
def run(controller):
    controller.reset()
    controller.configure_voltage_window(2.8, 4.2)

    requested = -1.500
    pulse_out = controller.pulse_current(requested, 10.0)

    controller.rest_for(30.0)
    controller.stop()

    return {
        "requested_current": requested,
        "actual_current": pulse_out if isinstance(pulse_out, (int, float)) else requested,
        "pulse_duration": 10.0,
        "rest_duration": 30.0,
        "status": "completed",
    }
